text: The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a laparoscopic stapler. The proximal and distal sides of the intestinal tract are transected, and 10mm transverse enterotomies are created at the mesenteric edges. A 45mm linear stapler is inserted into each hole along the posterior wall of both intestinal tracts, closed, and fired. The entry hole is partially closed with a 30mm stapler and completely closed with a 60mm stapler, completing the intracorporeal suture-less stapled end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the dorsal side of the end-to-end anastomosis, confirming that the orientation of the mesentery is consistent on both the proximal and distal sides. The diameter of the anastomosis appears well secured.
Laparoscopic left colectomy was performed. The placement of the trochars is as shown in the figure. I will show you a video of the anastomosis procedure.

Following the transection of the proximal and distal sides of the intestinal tract with a laparoscopic stapler, endocyanine green is injected intravenously. Laparoscopic near-infrared observation confirms good blood flow. Ten millimeter transverse enterotomies are created at the mesenteric edges of the stapling lines on both the transverse colon and descending colon.

The surgeon inserts each jaw of a 45mm linear stapler into the holes along the posterior wall of both intestinal tracts, closes, and fires the device to perform the first suturing. The interior of the anastomosis is inspected for bleeding. The assistant grasps the proximal and distal ends of the suture line, using an Endo GIA reinforced reload for easier handling. The entry hole is partially closed with a 30mm stapler to prevent anastomosis stenosis, followed by complete closure with a 60mm stapler, ensuring all layers are sutured.

The intracorporeal sutureless stapled end-to-end anastomosis is completed. The anastomosis site shows an end-to-end connection with the mesentery oriented similarly on both the proximal and distal sides.

The postoperative course was uneventful, with the first flatus on the first postoperative day, dietary intake on the second postoperative day, and hospital discharge on the fifth postoperative day. First, this is an end-to-end anastomosis and the range of mobilization is less than that of previous anastomosis, and it is less likely to get tension. Second, it is physiological because the mesentery is oriented in the same direction. Third, anastomosis time is reduced because no hand zone suture in the abdominal cavity is required. In conclusion, we believe that this anastomosis technique will be an alternative to the conventional side-to-side anastomosis.
